Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic most of the time. I'd have bursts where I'd try to get things together, maybe study for a few hours, but then I'd drift off and lose momentum. I'm a visual learner, so I'd try to make diagrams or watch videos, but organizing when and how I studied? That rarely happened in any consistent way. I'd often find myself cramming or just doing the bare minimum to get by.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really. Deadlines were the main thing that got me moving—if something was due, I'd suddenly find the energy to study. Otherwise, I'd let days slip by without really touching course material. Some weeks I'd put in decent hours, other weeks almost nothing. It depended a lot on how I was feeling emotionally, which wasn't always great.
Q3: Usually it was because I remembered an assignment was coming up or I saw a notification on my phone. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, but I wasn't the type to browse around proactively. If there wasn't something pressing, I'd probably not bother logging in at all. The LMS felt more like a chore than a tool, if I'm being honest.
Q4: There were a few weeks where I got into a rhythm of checking the discussion boards and watching lecture videos. It happened when I was feeling a bit more stable emotionally and had fewer distractions. But that routine never really stuck for long—something would come up, or I'd just lose interest and fall back into old habits. It never became automatic for me the way it seemed to for other students.
Q5: The videos were the most useful for me because I learn better when I can see things visually. I'd pause them, rewind, and try to sketch out what was being explained. The discussion posts were also helpful sometimes because seeing other people's questions made me realize I wasn't the only one confused. Those two things probably helped me more than anything else on the platform.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the reading materials and any extra resources that weren't directly tied to assignments. I just didn't have the energy or focus to go through dense text when I was already struggling to keep up. If it wasn't going to show up on an exam or assignment, I figured it wasn't worth my limited time. I know that's not the best approach, but that's where I was at.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to my grade or if it helped me understand something I was genuinely stuck on. I needed to feel like it was going to make a difference, otherwise I'd just zone out. The mindfulness stuff they introduced actually helped me feel a bit more present sometimes, which made certain activities feel more worthwhile than they would have otherwise.
Q8: Optional activities felt like extras I couldn't afford to spend energy on. With everything else going on and my limited resources, I had to prioritize what was required. Required stuff felt like pressure, but at least I knew I had to do it. Optional things just felt like nice ideas that I'd never actually get around to doing, and that made me feel a bit guilty sometimes.
Q9: Yeah, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download slides or take screenshots of diagrams and then study from those on my phone or just printed notes when I could. I didn't always have reliable internet at home, so working offline was sometimes the only option. The LMS wouldn't show any of that time I spent reviewing materials away from my computer.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be sitting with my notes, trying to work through problems or memorize concepts, and none of that would register anywhere online. I'd spend hours going over printed diagrams or rewriting notes by hand because that's how I process information best. The system only sees clicks and logins, not the actual thinking and studying happening in my room.
Q11: Definitely. I'd often watch a video once, maybe take some notes or screenshots, and then never go back to it on the LMS. I'd study from what I'd saved instead. It was just easier and didn't require me to be online. So my actual engagement with that material was way higher than what the logs would show.
Q12: There were stretches where I just felt really sad and disconnected from everything, including the course. When that happened, even logging in felt like too much effort. I'd fall behind, and then catching up seemed impossible, which made me feel worse. It was a cycle that was hard to break out of, especially with low resilience to bounce back from setbacks.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was stressed or tired, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. If I had work stuff or personal issues piling up, coursework took a backseat. Confusion also played a role—if I didn't understand something, I'd sometimes just avoid it rather than struggle through. The fatigue made it hard to engage meaningfully even when I did log in.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd open materials and just stare at them without really absorbing anything. I'd scroll through slides or let a video play while my mind was somewhere else entirely. Technically I was 'engaged' according to the system, but mentally I was checked out. Those sessions didn't help me learn much at all.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially when I was feeling down or overwhelmed. I knew certain assignments were important, but I'd convince myself I'd do them later when I felt better. That 'later' often became the night before the deadline. It's frustrating because I knew what I should be doing, but I just couldn't make myself do it in the moment.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that consistently got me to use the LMS. Without a deadline looming, I'd drift and procrastinate. But when something was due soon, I'd suddenly be logging in, watching videos, checking discussion posts—whatever I needed to get the work done. My whole engagement pattern was driven by urgency rather than genuine interest or routine.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, but not always in a good way. Positive feedback gave me a temporary boost, but if I got criticism or a lower grade than expected, it would knock me down for a while. I'd sometimes avoid checking my grades because I was afraid of how it would make me feel. The mindfulness practice helped a bit with handling those emotional reactions.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but it didn't really accommodate someone like me who struggles with consistency. Everything was laid out, which was helpful, but I needed more flexibility or check-ins to keep me on track. The weekly modules felt relentless when I fell behind—there was no easy way to catch up without feeling overwhelmed by how much I'd missed.
Q19: The visual materials made engagement easier for me since that's how I learn best. But the amount of reading and text-heavy content made it harder. Also, having limited resources meant I couldn't always access things when I needed to, which created barriers. A simpler, more visual-focused design would have helped me stay engaged more consistently.
Q20: I think those students might be like me—logging in, clicking around, maybe watching videos, but not actually producing work because they're struggling internally. High activity doesn't mean high productivity. You can be present online without being mentally present. Sometimes you're just going through the motions hoping something will click.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me in some ways. My exam scores and assignment grades were decent even though I wasn't always active on the LMS. I did a lot of studying offline and in ways that didn't show up in the logs. So yeah, low LMS activity doesn't necessarily mean a student isn't learning—they might just be doing it differently.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in, what you clicked on, and how long you spent on certain pages. But they miss the quality of that engagement—whether you were actually focused or just zoned out. They also miss all the offline studying, the emotional struggles that affect learning, and the real effort that goes into understanding material outside the platform.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine effort to understand it. It's not just about showing up or clicking through materials—it's about being mentally present and invested. For me, true engagement happened in bursts when I could push past the sadness and actually connect with the content. Those moments were rare but meaningful.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled emotionally throughout the course, or how the mindfulness intervention actually helped me cope sometimes. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying from printed notes or the internal battles I fought just to submit assignments. My engagement was complicated by things that don't show up in any log—feeling sad, having limited resources, and trying to keep going despite low resilience.
